text: The surgeon performs an emergency laparoscopic high anterior resection, starting with the blunt separation of the severely inflamed sigmoid colon and small bowel loops from the anterior abdominal wall. Care is taken to avoid iatrogenic bowel injury. Upon entering the abscess cavity, multiple locules of pus are broken down and aspirated.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and identifies a severely inflamed sigmoid colon adherent to the anterior abdominal wall, with small bowel loops also adherent to the inflamed segment. Blunt dissection is used to carefully separate the sigmoid colon and small bowel loops from the abdominal wall, avoiding iatrogenic injury. Upon entering the abscess cavity, multiple locules of pus are broken down and aspirated.

During the dissection, multiple pericolic and intra-loop locules of pus are identified. The surgeon navigates the challenging operative field, noting the risk of iatrogenic injury due to the proximity and adherence of bowel loops to the abscess cavity. The inflamed tissues are prone to bleeding, necessitating careful blunt dissection to ensure safety.
During the dissection, multiple pericolic and intra-loop locules of pus are identified. The inflamed tissues are visibly prone to bleeding, highlighting the challenges of the procedure.

The surgeon approaches the inferior mesenteric pedicle medially, maintaining dissection away from the inflamed colonic wall and high on the pedicle to identify structures more easily. The dissection progresses into the pelvis, separating the mesorectum from the presacral fascia to mobilize the upper rectum.

The surgeon approaches the inferior mesenteric pedicle medially, maintaining dissection away from the inflamed colonic wall and high on the pedicle to identify structures more easily. The dissection progresses into the pelvis, separating the mesorectum from the presacral fascia to mobilize the upper rectum.

The surgeon divides the colon at the recto sigmoid junction using an endo stapler, revealing a healthy, well-vascularized rectal stump with brisk arterial bleeding. Blunt separation of the colonic mesentery is performed to identify the sigmoid vessels, encountering troublesome bleeding from one of the sigmoid branches. Attempts to control the bleeding with diathermy and haemlocks are initially unsuccessful.
The surgeon uses an endo stapler to divide the colon at the recto sigmoid junction. The rectal stump appears healthy and well vascularized, indicated by brisk arterial bleeding.

The surgeon carefully performs blunt separation of the colonic mesentery to identify the sigmoid vessels. Troublesome bleeding from one of the sigmoid branches occurs, with initial attempts to control the bleeding using diathermy and haemlocks proving unsuccessful.

The surgeon attempts to achieve hemostasis using a harmonic scalpel, but the bleeding worsens. Quick action is taken to control the situation, and hemostasis is finally achieved with an intercorporeal transfixion stitch.

The surgeon attempts to control bleeding using a harmonic scalpel, but the situation worsens. Quick action is taken to achieve hemostasis with an intercorporeal transfixion stitch, visibly securing the bleeding site.

The surgeon continues dissection of the inferior mesenteric pedicle, navigating through the bulky and inflamed mesentery. The dissection is kept high on the pedicle to avoid retroperitoneal structures. An endo stapler is used to divide the inferior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon continues dissection of the bulky, inflamed mesentery around the inferior mesenteric pedicle, maintaining a high plane to avoid retroperitoneal structures. An endo stapler is used to divide the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon identifies an enlarged sigmoid vein, likely due to the inflammatory process, and divides it using hemlock clips.
The surgeon identifies an enlarged sigmoid vein, likely due to inflammation, and divides it using hemlock clips.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the descending colon along Todd's fascia, reaching the lower border of the splenic flexure. The colon is delivered through an enlarged incision in the left lower quadrant, revealing a large perforation defect. The sigmoid resection is completed externally by dividing the descending colon. An end colostomy is formed in the left lower quadrant at the specimen extraction site. After re-establishing pneumoperitoneum, the orientation of the colon and its mesentery is verified to ensure no twists or tension. A thorough washout is conducted, and a corrugated drain is placed in the pelvis.
